article_meta.title: Former President Donald Trump speaks at a campaign rally on April 27, 2023, in Manchester, New Hampshire

reasoning: The model correctly identifies two instances of author-level bias in the author's own prose, without flagging any quoted text. Both flagged items are genuinely in the author's voice and represent editorial judgment. The bias type labels are reasonable though debatable. However, the model misses several other instances of author bias in this article, such as 'dramatic story' (Subjective Qualifying Adjective/Word Choice), 'late Cuban despot Fidel Castro' ('despot' is loaded Word Choice), 'Breitbart's vague and unverified claim' (Subjective Qualifying Adjectives), 'Trump added more color to the story' (Spin - interpretive framing), and 'right-wing website Breitbart News' (Subjective Qualifying Adjective). The article is marked as CNN Opinion, which complicates the analysis somewhat, but there are still clear instances of loaded author prose beyond what was flagged.

custom_reasoning.attribution_rule_compliance: Both flagged items are clearly in the author's own prose, not in quoted text. No attribution rule violations.
custom_reasoning.false_positive_rate: Both flagged items are defensible as genuine bias in the author's voice. 'No reason to believe him' is an editorial judgment, and 'people should treat his story with great skepticism' is a direct opinion/recommendation. These are reasonable flags.
custom_reasoning.false_negative_rate: The model misses several clear instances: 'dramatic story' (loaded adjective), 'late Cuban despot Fidel Castro' (loaded word choice - 'despot'), 'Breitbart's vague and unverified claim' (subjective qualifying adjectives), 'right-wing website Breitbart News' (labeling), and potentially 'added more color' (interpretive verb). These are notable omissions.
custom_reasoning.bias_type_accuracy: The first item labeled 'Slant' could more precisely be 'Opinion Statements Presented as Fact' or 'Spin'. The second labeled 'Opinion Statements Presented as Fact' is reasonable. The labels are defensible but not perfectly precise - 'Slant' refers to structural de-emphasis of information in the article, which doesn't quite fit the first example.
